statement: Django uses MTV (Model-Template-View), NOT MVC. The Django 'View' is the controller — it handles business logic and selects templates. The 'Template' is the presentation layer. Models are Django's ORM layer with built-in validation. Key philosophy: batteries included — ORM, auth, admin, forms, caching, sessions all built in. UNSAID: Django's 'fat models, thin views' pattern means business logic belongs in model methods, not in views. Views that directly manipulate model data without model methods are hard to test and reuse.

Django ORM uses an Active Record pattern — each model instance represents a DB row. QuerySets are lazy: they don't hit the DB until evaluated (iteration, slicing, len(), bool()). This means chaining .filter().exclude().order_by() doesn't query the DB. UNSAID: QuerySets are ALSO cached — calling list(qs) evaluates and caches; qs[0] re-evaluates. This leads to the classic N+1 problem: iterating over objects and accessing related objects in a loop fires one query per iteration. select_related() and prefetch_related() are the fix.

The N+1 query problem: fetching 100 blog posts and then accessing post.author in a template fires 101 queries (1 for posts + 1 per post for author). Fix: Post.objects.select_related('author') for ForeignKey/OneToOne (SQL JOIN). Post.objects.prefetch_related('tags') for ManyToMany/reverse FK (separate query + Python join). Detection: django-debug-toolbar shows duplicate queries. django-silk profiles them. UNSAID: prefetch_related with a custom queryset uses Prefetch() object. Extraction signal: any view doing DB access inside a template loop is likely N+1.

Django signals (pre_save, post_save, post_delete) are synchronous and fire in the same transaction. They're used for side effects: audit logging, cache invalidation, sending emails. UNSAID: signals are invisible coupling — no type checker can trace them. post_save signal calling external APIs will delay saves and fail if the API is down. post_save fires even within transactions that will be rolled back — the signal handler may see data that never ends up in the DB. Best practice: use on_commit() for side effects that must only happen if the transaction commits.

Django migrations track schema changes as Python code. makemigrations generates them; migrate applies them. Migrations must be committed to version control — they ARE the schema history. UNSAID: squashmigrations compresses migration history for large projects. Data migrations (RunPython) mix schema and data changes — dangerous in production if the migration times out or fails halfway. Zero-downtime migration pattern: (1) add nullable column, (2) deploy and backfill, (3) make non-nullable. Extraction signal: migrations with direct SQL (RunSQL) indicate the ORM couldn't express the change.

Django model managers (Manager) encapsulate QuerySet logic. Custom managers add domain-specific query methods: Article.published.all() instead of Article.objects.filter(status='published'). Using get_queryset() override restricts the default queryset globally. UNSAID: overriding get_queryset() in the default manager affects admin, related managers, and all queries — use a separate manager for restricted views. Extraction signal: repeated .filter(is_active=True) or similar boilerplate across views is a sign that a custom manager is needed.

Django uses a single settings.py by convention, but production projects should split settings: base.py (common), local.py (development overrides), production.py (production config). The DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE environment variable selects which to use. SECRET_KEY, DATABASE_URL, and API keys must come from environment variables, never be hardcoded. UNSAID: django-environ or python-decouple are common libraries for reading env vars with type coercion. Extraction signal: SECRET_KEY hardcoded in settings.py is a security vulnerability and indicates the project is not production-ready.

Django REST Framework serializers are the layer between Python objects and JSON. ModelSerializer auto-generates fields from a Model. UNSAID: serializers run validation in two phases — field-level (validate_<fieldname>) then object-level (validate()). read_only_fields prevents clients from overwriting server-set fields (created_by, updated_at). SerializerMethodField for computed fields. The create() and update() methods must be explicitly overridden for nested writable serializers — DRF doesn't auto-handle nested creates. Extraction signal: DRF serializers with nested writable data that don't override create()/update() will silently ignore nested object changes.

DRF ViewSets combine CRUD operations into a single class. ModelViewSet provides list, create, retrieve, update, partial_update, destroy. Routers auto-generate URL patterns from ViewSets. UNSAID: the get_queryset() method is the correct place to filter data by the current user — overriding it ensures every action (list, retrieve, update, delete) is filtered. Not overriding get_queryset() and relying only on object-level permissions leaves list endpoints leaking all users' data. Extraction signal: ViewSets with permission_classes but without user-scoped get_queryset() likely expose cross-user data in list views.

Django's cache framework supports multiple backends: Redis (django-redis), Memcached, filesystem, in-memory. Cache is configured in CACHES setting. cache.set(key, value, timeout), cache.get(key), cache.delete(key). Django's per-view cache (@cache_page) and per-site cache are higher-level. UNSAID: cache invalidation is the hardest part — stale cache causes data inconsistency bugs that are hard to reproduce. Pattern: cache-aside (read from cache, fallback to DB, write to cache). django-cacheops provides ORM-level automatic invalidation. Extraction signal: applications with cache.set() but no cache.delete() on data mutations will serve stale data.

Django's security defaults: CSRF protection (CSRFMiddleware) is on by default for all POST/PUT/DELETE forms. Same-origin cookie policy prevents CSRF. XSS protection via template auto-escaping. Clickjacking protection via X-Frame-Options header. UNSAID: @csrf_exempt is a security downgrade — use it only for webhook endpoints that can't send CSRF tokens (and verify a shared secret instead). Django's DEBUG=True in production disables security headers and exposes tracebacks to browsers — a critical misconfiguration. Extraction signal: DEBUG=True in production settings is a critical security issue that must be caught in deployment checks.

Django + Celery integration: create a celery.py in the project package, call app.config_from_object('django.conf:settings', namespace='CELERY'), and autodiscover tasks with app.autodiscover_tasks(). UNSAID: Django ORM can be used in Celery tasks because Celery workers load Django settings via DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE. But tasks must call django.setup() if run outside the Celery worker. Database connections are NOT shared between Django request threads and Celery workers — each worker has its own connection pool. close_old_connections() should be called at the start of long-running tasks to avoid using stale connections. Extraction signal: Celery tasks that use Django models without proper setup cause 'Apps aren't loaded yet' errors.

Django wraps each request in an atomic transaction by default (ATOMIC_REQUESTS=True). UNSAID: this means a slow request holds a DB transaction open for its entire duration, blocking other queries that need row locks. For requests that do heavy computation and then a final write, use transaction.atomic() only around the write block. Also: exceptions inside @transaction.atomic rollback the transaction — raising an exception in a signal handler rolls back the entire parent transaction. Side effects (emails, external API calls) inside transactions are dangerous because they execute even if the transaction is later rolled back. on_commit() defers code until after successful commit.

Django admin is a powerful auto-generated CRUD interface. Customization points: list_display (columns), list_filter (sidebar filters), search_fields (search bar), readonly_fields, fieldsets (layout). Admin actions (bulk operations). Custom admin views via get_urls(). UNSAID: Django admin is NOT a user-facing product interface — it's a developer/superuser tool. Using admin for customer-facing workflows creates UX, permission, and audit problems. Admin bypasses custom model methods and validation by default — direct DB writes via admin can corrupt application invariants. Extraction signal: business-critical workflows routed through admin are an architectural smell.

Django middleware is a stack of callables that wrap the request/response cycle. New-style middleware: a class with __init__(get_response) and __call__(request). process_view(), process_exception(), process_template_response() hooks for specific phases. UNSAID: middleware order matters — AuthenticationMiddleware must run before SessionMiddleware consumer code, because auth reads from session. Middleware that modifies request.user must run before any middleware or view that reads request.user. Common pattern: RequestID middleware adds a unique ID per request for distributed tracing — must be first in MIDDLEWARE list. Extraction signal: middleware added at the bottom of MIDDLEWARE list that needs to run before auth will silently fail.

Django TestCase wraps each test in a transaction that is rolled back after the test — fast but means signals and on_commit() don't fire. TransactionTestCase truncates tables after each test — slower but tests real transaction behavior. UNSAID: mixing TestCase and TransactionTestCase in the same test suite causes ordering issues. factory_boy is the preferred fixture library over Django fixtures (JSON files) because fixtures are brittle with schema changes. pytest-django provides a django_db mark that's more idiomatic with pytest. Extraction signal: projects that use loaddata for test fixtures will have failing tests after every migration that changes the data shape.

Django Channels extends Django to handle WebSockets, long-polling, and background tasks via ASGI. Channels replaces the WSGI server for async-capable deployments. Channel layers (Redis backend) enable broadcasting to groups of connected clients. UNSAID: Django Channels is NOT async-native for ORM access — sync_to_async() wrapper is needed for database access inside consumers. Running ORM queries directly in async consumers blocks the event loop. Extraction signal: Channels consumers that call ORM methods without sync_to_async will appear to work in tests but will block the ASGI event loop under load.

Django's deployment checklist (manage.py check --deploy) validates: DEBUG=False, SECRET_KEY strength, ALLOWED_HOSTS, HTTPS settings (SECURE_SSL_REDIRECT, HSTS), cookie security (SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE, CSRF_COOKIE_SECURE). UNSAID: ALLOWED_HOSTS=['*'] in production is a Host header injection vulnerability. Static files must be served by the web server or CDN (not Django's development server) — collectstatic gathers them to STATIC_ROOT. WhiteNoise is the simplest solution for serving static files from Django in production without a separate web server. Extraction signal: applications deploying to production without running manage.py check --deploy have unknown security misconfigurations.

Django's permission system: model-level permissions (add, change, delete, view) auto-generated per model. @login_required, @permission_required decorators for views. DRF: permission_classes on ViewSets. Object-level permissions require a custom backend or django-guardian. UNSAID: model-level permissions check if a user CAN perform the action at all; object-level permissions check if they can perform it on SPECIFIC instance. Django admin uses model-level permissions only — users with 'change_user' permission can change ALL users including superusers, a privilege escalation risk. Extraction signal: admin sites where staff users have model-level edit permissions but shouldn't see all records need object-level permissions.

Django 5.x new features: (1) Facet filters in admin — admin.ModelAdmin.show_facets controls per-field facet counts in the sidebar; (2) Field groups in forms — rendered with {% formgroup %} tag for semantic HTML; (3) Database-generated columns — GeneratedField(expression=..., output_field=..., db_persist=True) maps to SQL GENERATED ALWAYS AS columns for computed fields maintained by the DB; (4) LoginRequiredMiddleware — replaces per-view @login_required with a project-wide default, with opt-out @login_not_required for public views. WHY GeneratedField matters: computed columns previously required model properties (Python-only) or custom signals to maintain DB columns. GeneratedField ensures the computation is in the DB and always consistent, even after bulk updates that bypass Python.

Django async views (Django 4.1+): define a view as 'async def my_view(request)' and Django will run it in an async context using ASGI. Async class-based views override async def get()/post(). Django's ORM remains synchronous by default — use sync_to_async() wrapper or the async QuerySet API (Django 4.1+ adds afilter(), aget(), acreate(), acount(), etc.) to avoid blocking the event loop. WHY async views matter: for views that make multiple external API calls, async allows concurrent fetching (asyncio.gather) in a single view, reducing response time from sum-of-calls to max-of-calls. UNSAID: mixing sync ORM calls and async views causes Django to emit a SynchronousOnlyOperation error when running under ASGI — this is intentional to prevent accidental event loop blocking. Extraction signal: Django projects running under ASGI (Daphne, Uvicorn) that use sync ORM calls in async views will fail with SynchronousOnlyOperation.

Django ORM N+1 deep patterns beyond basic select_related: (1) Reverse FK relations — Post.objects.prefetch_related('comments') prefetches all comments in one query; (2) Nested relations — prefetch_related('comments__author') chains prefetches; (3) Annotations instead of properties — annotate(comment_count=Count('comments')) adds a computed field in SQL, avoiding Python-side aggregation loops; (4) Only/defer — .only('id', 'title') fetches fewer columns, avoiding expensive text fields; (5) values_list() for bulk ID extraction without model instantiation overhead. UNSAID: prefetch_related with a filtered Prefetch() object is the only way to apply WHERE conditions to prefetched relations — Post.objects.prefetch_related(Prefetch('comments', queryset=Comment.objects.filter(approved=True))). Using Python filtering after prefetch defeats the purpose (all data is fetched then filtered in Python).

Django migration strategy for zero-downtime deployments: the naive approach (add non-nullable column → deploy) causes downtime because the migration adds the column with NOT NULL before the new code is deployed. Safe three-phase pattern: Phase 1 — add nullable column (no default required, existing rows get NULL), deploy; Phase 2 — backfill with a data migration or management command, add application-level default in code; Phase 3 — add DB-level NOT NULL constraint (fast if all rows filled) and remove nullable. UNSAID: Django's migration framework does NOT automatically detect that removing a field from the model is a breaking change for running instances — the old code reading the dropped column will get AttributeError until fully redeployed. Extraction signal: projects that run migrate before deploying new code can safely add nullable columns; projects that deploy code before migrate can safely read new nullable columns as None.

Django signals anti-patterns: (1) Connecting signals in models.py at module level — signals are connected every time the module is imported, leading to duplicate handler calls if the module is imported multiple times (rare but possible in test setups); correct location is AppConfig.ready(); (2) Signals for business logic — signals create invisible coupling that breaks the 'explicit is better than implicit' principle; business logic in signal handlers is untestable in isolation; (3) post_save for external side effects — the signal fires inside the transaction; if the external call fails, the transaction is already committed; use on_commit() callback to defer external calls until after commit; (4) Circular signal chains — A.post_save triggers B.save() which triggers B.post_save which triggers A.save(). UNSAID: Django doesn't protect against signal recursion — add a 'dispatch_uid' parameter to prevent duplicate registration and guard against circular chains with a flag.

Django REST Framework advanced patterns: (1) Custom authentication — implement BaseAuthentication.authenticate() returning (user, token) tuple or None; (2) Throttling — AnonRateThrottle/UserRateThrottle with DEFAULT_THROTTLE_RATES in settings; custom throttles override get_cache_key(); (3) Filtering — DjangoFilterBackend (django-filter package) for URL query parameter filtering; SearchFilter for full-text; OrderingFilter for sort; (4) Pagination — PageNumberPagination, LimitOffsetPagination, CursorPagination (most scalable — uses opaque cursor, consistent results even with concurrent inserts); (5) Versioning — URLPathVersioning (v1/, v2/) or AcceptHeaderVersioning. UNSAID: CursorPagination requires the queryset to have a stable ordering — without ORDER BY, cursor results are non-deterministic and will return duplicates or skip rows. Extraction signal: DRF list views with LimitOffset pagination on large tables without a DB index on the ordering column will degrade to full table scans.

Django + Celery production integration patterns: (1) Use django-celery-results to store task results in the Django DB — avoids a separate Redis result backend; (2) Use django-celery-beat for periodic tasks stored in DB (editable via admin) instead of static celerybeat_schedule; (3) Task idempotency — Celery guarantees at-least-once delivery; tasks that write to DB must use update_or_create or check existence before creating; (4) Task chaining — chain(fetch_data.s(), process.s(), save.s()) builds a pipeline; chord() for fan-out/fan-in; (5) close_old_connections() at task start prevents using stale DB connections that were created before the worker forked. WHY at-least-once matters: a task can execute twice if the worker crashes after completing but before acknowledging the broker. Idempotent tasks are safe to retry; non-idempotent tasks (send email, charge card) need deduplication logic with a unique task ID stored in the DB.
